diagnoses.clinical_rationale: Definitive Hashimoto pattern: TSH 7.2 uIU/mL (ref 0.4-4.0), Free T3 2.0 pg/mL (below range), Free T4 0.8 ng/dL (low-normal), TPO antibodies 68 IU/mL (ref <9), TgAb 4.2 IU/mL (ref <1.0). The antibody-positive thyroid dysfunction combined with positive ANA (1:160 speckled pattern), gluten sensitivity, and strong maternal family history of Hashimoto confirms autoimmune thyroid disease. Elevated hs-CRP 3.1 mg/L reflects autoimmune-driven inflammation. Macrocytic anemia (Hgb 11.2, MCV 104) is consistent with thyroid-driven B12 deficiency and iron depletion.

diagnoses.diagnosis: Multi-Nutrient Deficiency Syndrome (Iron, B12, Vitamin D, Zinc, Magnesium)
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: Severe iron deficiency: serum iron 38 µg/dL (ref 60-170), ferritin 8 ng/mL (ref 12-150), TIBC 420 µg/dL (elevated — compensatory). Macrocytic anemia (Hgb 11.2, MCV 104) indicates concurrent B12 deficiency (188 pg/mL, below range). Vitamin D 16 ng/mL deficient. Zinc 58 µg/dL low. RBC magnesium 3.8 mg/dL (ref 4.2-6.8) low. Multiple nutrient deficiencies in a vegetarian with gluten sensitivity indicates malabsorption from subclinical celiac or autoimmune gut involvement.

diagnoses.diagnosis: Elevated Cardiovascular Risk with Autoimmune Lipoprotein Elevation
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: LDL 142 mg/dL, ApoB 112 mg/dL, LDL particle number 1320, non-HDL 164 mg/dL, and total cholesterol 222 mg/dL are all elevated. Lipoprotein(a) 42 mg/dL (ref <30) adds independent, non-modifiable cardiovascular risk. Hypothyroidism directly raises LDL. Prolactin 28.0 ng/mL is elevated — likely from oral contraceptive use but warrants monitoring. ANA 1:160 speckled pattern requires rheumatology follow-up to assess for evolving systemic autoimmune process.

foods_to_eat.food: Lentils and cooked legumes (lentejas, frijoles bien cocidos)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Best vegetarian source of non-heme iron. Cooking reduces lectins and phytates that inhibit iron absorption. Also provides B vitamins and zinc.

foods_to_eat.food: Spinach and dark leafy greens with vitamin C (espinaca con limón o guayaba)
foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 2 cups daily, always paired with vitamin C source
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Iron-rich plant foods. Consuming with vitamin C converts ferric to ferrous iron, increasing absorption 3-fold. Your ferritin at 8 ng/mL requires aggressive dietary iron support.
Each Brazil nut provides 70-90 mcg selenium. Selenium reduces TPO antibody levels, improves T4-to-T3 conversion, and is the most evidence-based dietary intervention for Hashimoto's.

foods_to_eat.food: Wild-caught fatty fish — salmon, sardines (salmón, sardinas)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Omega-3s reduce autoimmune inflammation, lower hs-CRP, and support the cardiovascular risk created by elevated LDL and Lp(a). Also provides vitamin D and B12.
Excellent zinc source (2-3 mg/serving). Your zinc at 58 µg/dL is below normal. Zinc is required for thyroid hormone synthesis and immune regulation.

foods_to_eat.food: Beets and beet greens (remolacha y sus hojas)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Iron, folate, and betaine — supports methylation important for B12 metabolism and reduces homocysteine risk.
Complete B12 source, selenium, and choline. B12 at 188 pg/mL is below range. Eggs are one of the best absorbable B12 sources for vegetarians.
Monounsaturated fats reduce LDL and improve lipid profiles. Also provides folate, B vitamins, and potassium.
Complete protein with iron, zinc, and magnesium. Gluten-free, making it ideal for your gluten sensitivity. Provides all essential amino acids for thyroid hormone synthesis.

foods_to_eat.food: Spearmint or peppermint tea (té de hierbabuena o menta)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Anti-inflammatory and supports digestive health. Gut microbiome health strongly influences autoimmune thyroid disease.

foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 1 oz daily between meals (not with thyroid medication)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Omega-3 ALA reduces cardiovascular inflammation and supports LDL particle quality. Important note: nuts contain goitrogens — consume between meals, not near levothyroxine if prescribed.

foods_to_eat.food: Bone broth or collagen-rich soups (caldo de hueso)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Provides glycine and proline that support gut lining integrity. Improving gut absorption is critical given your multiple nutrient deficiencies.
Polyphenols and anthocyanins reduce autoimmune inflammation. Vitamin C in berries also enhances iron absorption from plant foods.

foods_to_eat.food: Coconut yogurt with probiotics (yogur de coco con probióticos)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Dairy-free probiotic source supports gut microbiome. Thyroid autoimmunity has strong gut-immune connections; improving gut health can reduce antibody levels.

foods_to_eat.food: Turmeric with black pepper (cúrcuma con pimienta negra)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Curcumin reduces autoimmune inflammation, lowers hs-CRP, and has been studied specifically in Hashimoto's for antibody reduction.

foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: You have documented gluten sensitivity. Gluten triggers intestinal inflammation and molecular mimicry with thyroid tissue, directly elevating TPO antibodies. Complete elimination is the most impactful dietary intervention for Hashimoto's.
Dairy proteins (casein) cross-react with gluten in many autoimmune individuals, perpetuating gut inflammation and nutrient malabsorption. Also interferes with thyroid medication absorption.

foods_to_avoid.food: Raw cruciferous vegetables — raw kale, raw broccoli (kale crudo, brócoli crudo)
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Always cook cruciferous vegetables; limit to 1 serving cooked daily
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Raw cruciferous vegetables contain goitrogens that inhibit thyroid iodine uptake. Always cook them — cooking inactivates goitrogenic compounds.

foods_to_avoid.food: Soy products — tofu, soy milk, edamame (tofu, leche de soya)
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Limit to 1-2 servings cooked soy per week; avoid soy near thyroid medications
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Soy isoflavones inhibit thyroid peroxidase and compete with iodine absorption. Given elevated TPO antibodies, minimize soy exposure.

foods_to_avoid.food: Refined sugar and commercial sweets (azúcar refinada, dulces industriales)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Sugar directly activates NF-kB inflammatory pathways that worsen autoimmunity. Raises hs-CRP. Your immune system is already overactivated.

foods_to_avoid.food: Seed oils high in omega-6 (aceites de girasol, maíz, soya)
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Replace with olive oil or coconut oil
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Excess omega-6 increases pro-inflammatory eicosanoids that worsen autoimmunity and oxidize LDL particles (LDL 142 mg/dL, Lp(a) 42 mg/dL).

foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Eliminate during active autoimmune flares; maximum 1 drink/week otherwise
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Increases intestinal permeability (leaky gut), worsening autoimmune triggers. Interferes with B12 absorption — critical given your B12 deficiency.

foods_to_avoid.food: High-calcium foods near iron supplement times
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Separate calcium-rich foods from iron supplements by 2 hours
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Calcium directly blocks iron absorption. With ferritin at 8 ng/mL, timing of supplements and foods is critical.

foods_to_avoid.food: Coffee near medication or iron supplement
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Wait 60 minutes after any thyroid or iron medication before coffee
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Tannins in coffee inhibit thyroid medication and iron supplement absorption by up to 50%.

foods_to_avoid.food: Ultra-processed foods and food additives (alimentos ultraprocesados)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Emulsifiers, preservatives, and artificial additives disrupt gut microbiome and increase intestinal permeability, worsening autoimmune activity.

exercise_recommendations.exercise_type: Moderate Cardio — continue running but reduce volume
exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Moderate exercise reduces autoimmune inflammation and supports weight maintenance. However, you are currently over-exercising (60-90 min daily HIIT) relative to your nutritional intake — excessive exercise elevates cortisol and worsens Hashimoto's. Reducing intensity is important.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: Given low hemoglobin (11.2 g/dL) and low ferritin (8 ng/mL), high-intensity exercise can worsen iron depletion. Monitor for excessive fatigue, heart racing, or light-headedness.

exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Resistance training increases bone density (important given family history of osteoporosis and your low calcium/phosphorus). Yoga reduces cortisol, which directly suppresses thyroid function when elevated.
Light daily movement improves lymphatic flow, reduces inflammation, and supports thyroid function without cortisol elevation. Given your desk job, this prevents prolonged sitting.

supplement_recommendations.timing: On empty stomach with vitamin C (e.g., 500mg vitamin C or fresh lemon juice); 2 hours away from dairy, calcium, and coffee
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Ferritin at 8 ng/mL is critically low and requires supplementation. Bisglycinate form has better absorption and fewer GI side effects than ferrous sulfate.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: Oral contraceptive does not interact. B12 supplementation should be taken at a different time. Recheck ferritin in 8 weeks.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Hierro bisglicinato available in pharmacies and health food stores across Latin America.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: B12 at 188 pg/mL is below normal with macrocytic anemia (MCV 104). Sublingual methylcobalamin bypasses gut absorption issues common in autoimmune conditions. Critical for neurological health and DNA synthesis.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No interactions with oral contraceptive. Very safe.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Methylcobalamin available in pharmacies in larger cities; cyanocobalamin is more widely available as alternative.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: 200 mcg daily selenium has been shown in randomized trials to reduce TPO antibody titers by 30-40% in Hashimoto's. Also supports T4-to-T3 conversion and reduces thyroid inflammation.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: Do not exceed 400 mcg/day total. Reduce if eating Brazil nuts daily.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Selenometionina available in pharmacies and health food stores.

supplement_recommendations.timing: With fat-containing meal, separate from thyroid medications by 4 hours if prescribed
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Vitamin D at 16 ng/mL is deficient — this directly worsens autoimmune activity by impairing regulatory T-cell function. Repletion to 50-70 ng/mL can reduce TPO antibodies and improve thyroid function.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No interactions with oral contraceptive at this dose.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Widely available. Vitamina D3 + K2 in health food stores; D3 alone in most pharmacies.

supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Zinc at 58 µg/dL is below normal. Zinc is required for thyroid hormone synthesis, immune regulation, and gut lining integrity. Deficiency worsens both Hashimoto's and iron absorption.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: Take with food. Avoid same time as iron supplement — space by 2 hours.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Zinc Picolinato available in health food stores; Zinc Gluconate in most pharmacies.

patient_summary: Your lab panel reviewed 74 markers across 6 panels, with 23 values outside normal range forming a clear pattern of autoimmune thyroid disease and multi-system nutrient depletion. TSH at 7.2 uIU/mL with positive TPO antibodies (68 IU/mL), positive TgAb, and low Free T3 confirms active Hashimoto thyroiditis — consistent with your mother's diagnosis. You also have a positive ANA (1:160, speckled) requiring rheumatology follow-up to monitor for broader autoimmune involvement. Compounding this, you have severe iron deficiency (ferritin 8 ng/mL) with resulting macrocytic anemia, B12 deficiency (188 pg/mL), low vitamin D (16 ng/mL), zinc deficiency, and depleted RBC magnesium — a pattern commonly seen when gluten-related gut inflammation impairs nutrient absorption. Your cardiovascular panel shows elevated LDL (142 mg/dL), Lp(a) (42 mg/dL), and ApoB — partly driven by your hypothyroidism. The most urgent first steps are: eliminating gluten completely, starting iron bisglycinate and sublingual B12 supplementation, beginning selenium and vitamin D3 to address the Hashimoto's root cause, and physician evaluation for thyroid hormone replacement. Over-exercising at 60-90 min daily HIIT is counterproductive at this stage — reducing to moderate-intensity exercise will support recovery. Repeat labs at 6-8 weeks to track thyroid antibodies, iron status, and B12.

system_summaries.summary: Macrocytic anemia is present: hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (low) with MCV 104 fL (elevated), consistent with B12 and/or folate deficiency. Iron deficiency anemia is also concurrent (ferritin 8 ng/mL, serum iron 38 µg/dL). This combined anemia pattern reflects both iron and B12 deficiency, both driven by poor absorption in the context of gluten sensitivity and autoimmune gut involvement.

Kidney function is normal. Calcium is borderline low at 8.4 mg/dL (ref 8.5-10.5), phosphorus is low at 2.3 mg/dL, and serum magnesium is low at 1.5 mg/dL. These deficiencies are consistent with the broader malabsorption pattern and increase the risk of bone mineral density loss — a concern given your family history of osteoporosis.

ALP is slightly elevated at 135 U/L — this can be seen with bone metabolism changes, liver stress, or malabsorption. Albumin and total protein are low-normal, consistent with reduced protein intake and absorption. ALT and AST are normal.

Your thyroid panel confirms Hashimoto thyroiditis: TSH 7.2 uIU/mL, Free T3 2.0 pg/mL (below range), TPO antibodies 68 IU/mL (markedly elevated), TgAb 4.2 IU/mL (elevated). Free T4 is at the lower boundary. Thyroid replacement therapy and selenium supplementation require physician discussion.

ANA is positive at 1:160 with speckled pattern. Speckled ANA at this titer, combined with confirmed Hashimoto's and elevated hs-CRP (3.1 mg/L), indicates active autoimmune processes. RF is normal, making rheumatoid arthritis less likely. However, rheumatology evaluation is recommended to assess for mixed connective tissue disease or other systemic autoimmunity.

LDL 142 mg/dL, ApoB 112 mg/dL, LDL particle number 1320, and non-HDL 164 mg/dL are elevated. Lipoprotein(a) at 42 mg/dL (ref <30) is independently elevated and cannot be lowered by diet — this is a genetic risk marker requiring physician awareness. HDL 58 mg/dL and triglycerides 108 mg/dL are favorable. Hypothyroidism is a primary driver of elevated LDL; treating thyroid disease will improve lipid markers.

Prolactin is elevated at 28.0 ng/mL, likely related to oral contraceptive use, though hypothyroidism can also elevate prolactin. This should be monitored. Reproductive hormones are otherwise normal. AMH 1.8 ng/mL indicates normal ovarian reserve — reassuring given fertility preparation goals. Thyroid treatment is important before conception planning.

Severe multi-nutrient deficiency: iron deficiency (serum iron 38, ferritin 8, TIBC 420), B12 deficiency (188 pg/mL), vitamin D deficiency (16 ng/mL), zinc deficiency (58 µg/dL), and low RBC magnesium (3.8 mg/dL). This constellation points to impaired absorption from gut inflammation — gluten elimination is the most important first step. Copper and selenium are normal, which is favorable.
